# Questions in Livestream 188

Live Stream #188: Turning to Spilling the Coffee

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=903" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:03</a> | Did Egyptian princes practice stick-fighting arts such as tahtib? | He said generally no. Those arts show up more with slaves or lower-status fighters, while princes were trained in chariotry, archery, and elite military skills. | He says Egyptian royals watched such fighting as entertainment but generally did not train in it themselves. Princes were expected to learn skills suited to elite command, especially chariotry, archery, spears, and javelins, while stick-fighting and grappling were associated more with slaves, indentured men, or lower-status performers. He also gives a practical reason: royal heirs should not be placed in contests where they might be seriously injured or killed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=1054" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:34</a> | How did the Mesopotamian transition from Akkadian to Aramaic affect what early archaeologists could know before cuneiform was deciphered? | He said Aramaic was already known, but the transition actually narrowed what could be learned because later Aramaic material did not preserve the older Akkadian cultural record. | He explains that Aramaic being readable did not give early archaeologists a fuller picture, because the change of language also involved a change of writing medium. Akkadian texts were commonly preserved on clay tablets, while Aramaic was often written on parchment, which survives poorly. As a result, the later readable language left fewer durable records, and real access to Mesopotamian culture expanded only after cuneiform was deciphered. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=1723" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:43</a> | Is the Merneptah Stele really a problem for the late date of the Exodus? | He argued no, because the stele's portrayal of Israel still fits a late-exodus model and does not require an earlier conquest date. | He argues that a late Exodus around 1265 BC with a forty-year wilderness period puts Israel in Canaan by about 1225 BC, making Merneptah's later encounter with Israel reasonable rather than problematic. He also stresses that the stele does not call Israel a nation but a migratory people group, which he sees as fitting early Israel better than a long-established monarchy. He adds that the presence of groups such as Hurrians in Canaan would be harder to explain if the conquest had happened two centuries earlier. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=2101" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:01</a> | Who is Frank Turek? | He identified Turek as a well-known apologist connected with CrossExamined.org and a large apologetics YouTube channel. | He answers the clarifying question by saying Frank Turek is a well-known apologist with the ministry CrossExamined.org. He describes Turek's YouTube channel as one of the larger apologetics channels, with more than 600,000 subscribers, and gives that background because the prior Merneptah Stele question had referenced Turek's short video. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=2165" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:05</a> | Is there any news on the Titus Kennedy complaint? | He said it was a privacy complaint rather than a copyright complaint, and YouTube had not taken the video down. | He corrects the premise by saying the complaint was technically a privacy complaint, not a copyright complaint. At the time of the livestream, the video was still up and YouTube had not acted further, which he interpreted as a possible sign that YouTube reviewed it and chose not to get involved. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=2397" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:57</a> | Are you going to play the new Indiana Jones game, or would it be too much like work? | He said it would probably feel too much like work to play on his own. | He says he enjoyed the Assassin's Creed playthrough because other people were playing along with him, but playing an Indiana Jones game by himself would probably feel like work rather than entertainment. He therefore would not volunteer to do it as a solo playthrough. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=3185" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:05</a> | Can you recommend any underrated novels or novelists? | He said underrated authors are hard to identify, but he named several authors he considers worthwhile. | He explains that underrated authors are difficult to recommend because, by definition, they often do not show up on a reader's radar. Instead, he names authors he considers good, including Robert Silverberg, Ernest Hemingway, C. S. Forester, Michael Crichton, and G. K. Chesterton, while distinguishing those from authors he thinks are overrated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=3731" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:11</a> | Why did God give the Israelites the promised land? | He framed it as part of the promise to Abraham: posterity, land, and relationship with God. | He says the promise is first a promise to Abraham rather than an isolated grant to later Israelites. The land belongs with the larger Abrahamic package of posterity, land, and relationship with God, so Israel receives the land because of its association with Abraham and the promise made to him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=3811" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:31</a> | How can Cain be a farmer if agriculture only developed a few thousand years ago and humans were hunter-gatherers before that? | He treated Cain and Abel as part of Genesis's farmer-versus-pastoralist pattern rather than a modern archaeological chronology claim. | He reads Cain and Abel as expressing the long-standing tension between settled farmers and pastoralists, especially as Israel entered a land where clan holdings, farming, and moving herds would have to coexist. He allows that Cain could have been a farmer or partly sedentary, while Abel need not represent fully developed pastoralism. He also says agriculture is older than the objection assumes, with early cultivation and domestication reaching much farther back than only a few thousand years. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=4171" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:31</a> | Would you please play the guitar? | He said no, explaining that he does not honor requests to play guitar on stream. | He says there are some questions and requests the channel will not answer or honor, including denomination questions and requests for him to play guitar. His reason is that the guitar is his personal composition instrument rather than a performance instrument, and he also notes the practical issue of apartment noise. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=4633" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:13</a> | What are your thoughts on the recent allegations against Michael Brown? | He said the allegations were very recent and urged waiting for the truth to come out rather than taking a side immediately. | He says the allegations had only surfaced days earlier, even though they concerned events said to be much older, so it would be irresponsible to act as though the facts were settled. He urges waiting for a third-party investigation and for the relevant information to emerge, noting from his own experience that accusations can spread faster than the truth. His answer is procedural rather than a judgment on guilt or innocence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=4811" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:11</a> | Is Gibeon a problem for the late Exodus date? | He said no; it is an unresolved issue, but not a disqualifying problem for the late-date model. | He says the lack of visible Late Bronze II remains at Gibeon is like several other 13th-century site problems: it may reflect site leveling, site movement, or another unresolved archaeological process. He compares it to other places where evidence is incomplete and argues that an unanswered site question does not override the broader evidence for a late Exodus date. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=4930" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:10</a> | Why is there a lack of Late Bronze II pottery at Jericho? | He said there is not a lack; Late Bronze IIB pottery is found across the site, which creates problems for some early-destruction arguments. | He says the premise is wrong: Late Bronze IIB pottery has been found all over Jericho and across excavations. The problem is that this pottery is not found together with Late Bronze IIB walls and buildings, which is why it often does not get integrated cleanly into reports. He connects this to Lorenzo Nigro's argument that site leveling may explain why the pottery remains while the corresponding infrastructure is missing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=5265" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:45</a> | Do you know about Wes Huff's recent debate with Billy Carson, and what do you think about it? | He had not seen the debate, so he only commented on what he had heard about the aftermath. | He says he did not watch the debate itself, but had heard that Huff did very well, that the debate was taken down because it was one-sided, and that Carson was trying to sue Huff over posted clips. He keeps the answer limited because he had only heard about the debate and its aftermath rather than seeing it firsthand. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=5408" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:08</a> | What does fundamentalist Christmas curriculum usually get wrong? | He said the common error is placing the Magi at the manger; in the text they visit a house when Jesus is already a young child. | He says the usual mistake is putting the Magi at the birth scene. In his reading, they came to a house later, probably one or two years after Jesus' birth, when Jesus was already a toddler. He also distinguishes that from some Catholic-style imagery that puts the birth in a cave, saying the manger should be understood more like a lean-to or hay-storage structure associated with a house, not a cave or animal dwelling. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=6138" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:18</a> | Are you going to discuss Lorenzo Nigro's paper on the Late Bronze II destruction evidence? | He said yes, eventually, because the 2023 paper states much more explicitly what the earlier work implied. | He says they do plan to cover the paper, though other work has delayed it. His summary is that Nigro's 2023 paper makes explicit what the 2018 paper had only implied: a Late Bronze IIB city existed, every excavator found evidence for it, and the destruction is inferred even though the city remains have largely disappeared. He notes that Nigro went back through earlier excavators' records and journals to support that conclusion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=6331" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:31</a> | What evidence is there for what language Job was originally written in? | He said Job may have come from an Aramaic or Syrian setting, but the evidence does not let him decide for sure. | He describes Job as an unusual book because it is set in Syria and Job is not an Israelite. He says it could have been written originally in Aramaic or been an Aramaic oral story later written down in Hebrew, but he cannot be certain. His best guess is that it is Iron Age, probably Iron Age II, material that came out of Syria and entered the Israelite corpus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=6511" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:31</a> | What commentary on Revelation would you recommend? | He recommended Grant Osborne's commentary as best for the overall dynamics of Revelation, even if not always for every detail. | He recommends Grant Osborne first because he thinks Osborne captures the overall dynamic and sense of Revelation well. He qualifies that it may not be the strongest commentary for every small detail, and he mentions using other large commentaries at times, but Osborne is the one he gravitates to for the book as a whole. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=6739" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:52:19</a> | Are the clan names in Genesis 5-11 the names that were remembered through long oral transmission? | He said they would be remembered names, but not necessarily perfect one-to-one forms because languages changed over time. | He frames the question around whether the names were recorded or preserved orally. If they were transmitted orally, they would be the remembered names, but he says language shifts over time would change their forms. In his view, the names in Genesis 5 and 11 are best understood as the remembered forms available when they were put down in writing, not as guaranteed exact unchanged originals. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=6859" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:19</a> | Was Akhenaten's Aten religion monotheistic, polytheistic, monolatrous, or henotheistic? | He called it monolatrous pantheism: everything was treated as a manifestation of the Aten, but only the Aten was worshiped. | He rejects the listed categories as insufficient and identifies Atenism as pantheistic and monolatrous. In his explanation, everything is a manifestation of the Aten, including people and objects, but worship is directed only to the Aten. That combination is why he calls it monolatrous pantheism rather than simple monotheism, polytheism, or henotheism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=6923" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:23</a> | Are there books that map faunal remains to Israelite borders and periods such as the Late Bronze and Iron Ages? | He said the work exists mostly in peer-reviewed papers rather than books, but he could not recall the specific papers. | He says he knows this type of work has been done and remembers it being referenced in class, but he cannot recall the authors or paper titles. His main distinction is publication form: the evidence is in peer-reviewed journal articles rather than a dedicated book, so he cannot give a specific book recommendation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=7004" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:44</a> | Was Potiphar a eunuch because he was captain of the guard in Genesis 39:1? | He said the biblical text does not say that, and Egyptian officials were not automatically made eunuchs. | He says older translations sometimes rendered the relevant official term as "eunuch," but in this context it means officer or commander. He contrasts Egypt with Mesopotamian palace practice, saying Egyptians did not make all male palace officials eunuchs; eunuchs guarded the harem, while Pharaoh's personal bodyguard remained intact. He identifies Potiphar as captain of Pharaoh's bodyguard, responsible first for protecting the king, not as a harem guard. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=7394" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:14</a> | Could you share your memories of the Apollo footage? | He said he has no memories of it because he was not born yet. | He treats the premise lightly and answers directly that he was not born when the Apollo landing footage aired. He says he was born about a year after the Apollo landing, so he does not have personal memories of seeing that footage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=7477" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:37</a> | Why do your Terah and Abraham synchronisms give slightly different Hammurabi dates than older material? | He said the synchronisms have been refined over time, with only modest adjustments as new data is folded into the chronology work. | He says the chronology has been updated as new synchronisms and data are added, so older videos may reflect earlier versions with a wider uncertainty range. The changes are not large in his view, often on the order of years or a couple of decades, and the current work has narrowed the error range. He also notes that Groundhog remains usable but still under development, with more data such as eponym lists still needing to be added. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=7860" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:11:00</a> | Were older chronologists ignorant of what computers were designed to do? | He said no; the issue was the size and difficulty of the chronology problem, especially uncertainty calculations. | He says chronology had become a field where many people still worked by hand and believed the problem was too large or uncertain for computers to handle. His point is that computers can calculate uncertainty, but only if the software is designed to handle difficult data types and the processing time required. He contrasts faster tools that limit the scope of data with Groundhog's slower approach to harder data. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=8069" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:29</a> | What is the name of the position where someone has lifetime research funding? | He called it a research millionaire: a fully endowed position that lets someone focus on research. | He explains that the position is funded through an endowment to a university, so the university receives the donation as a nonprofit institution. The researcher would still be paid a taxable salary from that endowed support, but the practical point is that the researcher can spend a lifetime focused on the work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=8199" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:16:39</a> | Did Midian have bad pasture lands? | He said Midian did not have great pasture lands, which helps explain why Moses would move into Sinai with the flocks. | He identifies Midian with the Arabian Peninsula and says that in the Late Bronze Age it was not as bad as today, but it still was not good pasture country. He says the region had already been affected by drying, so Moses likely had to move into Sinai where seasonal rains still provided pasture for the flocks. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=8339" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:59</a> | Why could the Passover animal come from either sheep or goats in Exodus 12:5? | He said the option made Passover possible for both wealthier and poorer households. | He explains that enslaved households were not all identical in wealth. Wealthier families might have sheep, while poorer or smaller families might have goats, so allowing either animal let everyone participate in Passover. Without the goat option, he says many poorer families would have been excluded from the protective rite. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=8487" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:27</a> | How does God judge the gods of Egypt in Exodus 12:12? | He interpreted the "gods" in that judgment as the decision-makers and powers of Egypt, not merely idols or spirit beings. | He argues that killing the firstborn would not meaningfully judge empty idols or spirit beings if those were the intended referent. He instead explains "god" as a term that can cover beings or authorities with life-and-death power in their sphere, including human judges or rulers. On that reading, the plague judges Egypt's ruling decision-makers and social powers that enslaved and oppressed Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=8820" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:00</a> | In Genesis 43:32, why was eating with Hebrews an abomination to Egyptians if the Hyksos were also Semites? | He answered after correcting the likely reference and said the issue was Egyptian disdain for shepherds and pastoralists. | The submitted reference was unclear, so he first asks for clarification and then answers on the assumption that Genesis 43:32 is intended. He says the Hyksos court included many Egyptians, so the issue was not simply Semite versus Semite. His explanation is that Egyptians despised shepherds and pastoralists because they were associated with fouling waterways, damaging fields, and occupying a low social class. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=8921" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:41</a> | Are the patriarchal names from Abraham to the twelve actual names or archetypal names? | He said we do not know for sure; they could be actual names, archetypal names, or wordplay, but the evidence is insufficient. | He gives a deliberately limited answer because the evidence does not let him decide. The names might be actual personal names, archetypal names, or "canting" names that play on or rhyme with earlier forms. Without more information, he says it is better not to guess beyond those possibilities. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=8982" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:42</a> | How should inspiration be explained to people who think of it as a channeled document? | He said inspiration works through God's providential shaping of a person's life, experiences, education, and circumstances rather than simple dictation. | He says the best explanation is that God shapes the whole life of the human author: parents, education, opportunities, failures, successes, work, reading, entertainment, and avoided experiences all contribute to the person who produces the text God intends. He contrasts that with a channeled or dictation model, which he describes as a demand for extra "magic" rather than a biblical view of God working through ordinary providence. He illustrates the same magical-thinking pattern with people who require a spectacular Gulf of Aqaba crossing rather than taking the text's wind language seriously, and he warns that people committed to that model may be hard to persuade. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=10491" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:54:51</a> | How were the Israelites affected, good and bad, by the Bronze Age Collapse? | He said they were overall winners because collapsing imperial powers gave Israel room to establish itself in Canaan. | He says the Israelites benefited because the collapse weakened or removed imperial neighbors such as Mitanni, the Hittites, and Egypt as active constraints. That gave Israel time and space to establish itself in Canaan, break up Amorite power, and gain a foothold in the land. He compares this to the Philistines, who also benefited by establishing themselves on the coastal plain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=10609" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:49</a> | If Joseph worshiped God in Genesis, would he also have worshiped the gods of the Hyksos? | He said we do not know; Joseph may have served within a Hyksos religious environment, but the data are too limited. | He says the text supports Joseph worshiping God, but the exact form of that worship is uncertain, including whether to identify it with El Shaddai or Elyon. He allows that Joseph may have done service connected with Hyksos gods, but says the available data are too limited to know how Israelite worship functioned in that Hyksos setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=10690" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:10</a> | If Israelites could not have anything with yeast during Passover, what about wine? | He said wine becomes an exception in later practice, with kosher wine heated to kill yeast, though earlier Israelite practice is harder to define. | He says later Jewish practice treats wine as acceptable for Passover by fermenting it and then heating it enough to kill the yeast, which is why kosher wine can be sweeter. For early Israelites, however, he says they probably did not understand fermentation microbiologically; grape juice simply became wine because yeast was naturally present on grape skins. He therefore distinguishes later koshering practice from what may have happened at the original Passover. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=11184" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:24</a> | How does "Everlasting Father" in Isaiah 9:6 better translate as "Father of Eternity"? | He pointed to the Hebrew construct relationship, explaining that the phrase is better read as "father of eternity." | He explains the phrase as a Hebrew construct relationship: the first noun means "father of," and the following noun is "eternity." Because the words are joined in that construct structure, he says the more literal rendering is "father of eternity" rather than treating "everlasting" as a simple adjective modifying "father." |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=11343" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:03</a> | Where is Pi-hahiroth? | He said we do not know exactly, though Egyptian documents mention it and it was probably south of the Pelusiac estuary among a cluster of towns. | He says the exact location is unknown, but Egyptian documents, probably including one of the Anastasi papyri, mention the place. He identifies the name as Egyptian and places it likely south of the Pelusiac estuary, near a cluster of towns and close to the Sea of Reeds. He also connects it geographically with Baal-zephon and the yam suf in the same general area. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=11484" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:24</a> | How do I hide from Wyatt fanboys? | He said he did not know, joking that the questioner had brought that problem on himself. | He treats the question lightly but says that if he knew how to avoid that kind of attention, he would say so. His more serious point is that such fans can become fanatical and obnoxious, and he does not have a clear strategy for handling that kind of response once someone has attracted it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=11563" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:43</a> | When does precaution become legalism? | He said precaution becomes legalism when it is made mandatory as a rule rather than treated as good advice. | He defines the dividing line as obligation: advice becomes legalism when it is turned into a rule everyone has to obey. He illustrates with fire and alcohol, where caution may be wise in some cases but an absolute rule can wrongly take away liberty. His point is that precaution can be good counsel without becoming a mandatory standard for others. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=12307" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:25:07</a> | Is it interesting that Genesis contains ancestral journeys while other religions remained native to their land? | He said yes; Genesis reflects a migratory people whose ancestors moved widely. | He says Genesis shows people getting around a great deal, which fits the religion and memory of a sojourning or migratory people. He connects Abraham and the Israelites with Amorite movements from Mesopotamia into Syria, Canaan, Egypt, and back toward Canaan, and says that wider Bronze Age and early Iron Age setting involved frequent movement of peoples. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=12983" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:23</a> | What are good sources for BC-era maps, routes, topography, and related data? | He recommended Barry Beitzel's Moody Bible Atlas, while noting that other Bible atlases exist. | He recommends Barry Beitzel's Moody Bible Atlas as his preferred source for ancient maps and related geographical data. He acknowledges other options, including an atlas by Alan Millard and Paul Lawrence, but says Beitzel is probably the better Bible atlas for this purpose. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=13064" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:37:44</a> | Is 1 Peter 3 connected to the Nephilim? | He said the question needed a more exact reference, but tentatively discussed 1 Peter 3:18-20 and treated the connection as not straightforward. | He first says the question needs a specific verse, then assumes 1 Peter 3:18-20 and discusses the "spirits in prison." He does not adopt Heiser's view that the Nephilim are supernatural beings or demon origins; instead, he understands them as fallen offspring of a priestly caste that broke vows. He says the Nephilim would be included among the disobedient old-world people, but he does not see the passage as clearly teaching Heiser's Nephilim connection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=13521" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:45:21</a> | Did Mary ride a donkey? | He said the Gospels do not say she did; it is probably an assumption from later retellings. | He says there is no Gospel verse that explicitly has Mary riding a donkey, so the familiar image is probably assumed rather than stated. He considers it plausible that Joseph owned a donkey or donkey cart because a carpenter would need to move materials, and Mary could have ridden in such a cart. He rejects confidence either way because the text itself does not say. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=13843" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:43</a> | Did the Magi likely ride camels? | He said it is hard to say; they likely rode something, but the animal is uncertain. | He says the text does not let him decide whether the Magi rode camels, horses, donkeys, or a mixed group of animals. He notes that a mixed group would be limited by its slowest animal, and contrasts that uncertainty with Joseph's situation, where a donkey cart would make practical sense for a carpenter who needed to move materials. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=14135" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:55:35</a> | Was David against Goliath more a shepherd tough-guy story than a Sunday-school little-guy story? | He said yes: David was a tough shepherd and skilled slinger, though still vulnerable if Goliath could close the distance. | He says David should be pictured as a tough shepherd who had faced lions and bears and as a skilled slinger, not merely as a helpless small boy. At the same time, David is still at a disadvantage if Goliath can close the distance. The contest turns on whether David can release a deadly slingstone before Goliath reaches him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=14269" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:57:49</a> | Was Goliath's `Am I a dog, that you come to me with sticks?` just mockery, or was something more going on? | He treated it as a taunt meant to stir courage before combat and to provoke movement from otherwise hesitant armies. | He says it was a taunt serving the larger function of representative combat: each army sends out a champion to give its own side courage to break the lines and fight. In his reading, both armies are afraid and stalled, and Goliath's bravado is partly for the Philistines' benefit. Once David kills Goliath, that intended morale effect reverses and the Philistines flee. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=14662" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:04:22</a> | Did Pontius Pilate die by old age or commit suicide? | He said we do not know; church tradition says suicide, but the records are not sufficient to confirm it. | He says the quick answer is that we do not know. Church tradition says Pilate committed suicide after the crucifixion, but the surviving records are not enough to establish that as reliable. We know Pilate stopped being governor of Judea shortly afterward, but not whether he was simply replaced, sent back to Rome, or died another way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=14738" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:05:38</a> | Was it fair for David to use a ranged weapon against Goliath? | He said yes; in one-on-one combat David could use a sling, and doing so carried real risk if he missed. | He says the rules of one-on-one combat allowed each fighter to use his weapon of choice, so David's sling was fair. David also accepted serious risk because he had little or no armor and no sidearms; if he missed or Goliath closed the distance, David would likely be killed easily. The fight therefore pits David's skill as a slinger against Goliath's infantry skill, with the outcome depending on who gets the effective strike first. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/aLsOi-ig6X4?t=14969" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:09:29</a> | Is site leveling of Late Bronze II Jericho part of a wider phenomenon in the southern Levant? | He said it may be; Jericho opens a line of inquiry that should not be ruled out at other sites. | He says many conquest-narrative sites have gaps or lack visible Late Bronze IIB phases or fortifications, and Jericho makes it harder to dismiss site leveling as a possible explanation. He gives Heshbon and Ai as examples where Late Bronze pottery without corresponding architecture could deserve renewed attention. His conclusion is cautious: archaeologists should be more careful about inferring that a city did not exist merely because later leveling removed much of the infrastructure. |
